title: The Casino Floor
subtitle: Expected value and why the house always wins
realWorldHook: A roulette bet pays 35-to-1 and feels like a jackpot waiting to happen — but the number only lands 1 spin in 38, so over time it drains you at a steady, guaranteed rate.
conceptSummary: Expected value = Σ(outcome × probability). Variance can pump you up early, but a negative EV makes the long-run slide inevitable — the same engine the house and the insurer both run.

scenario.role: You walk onto a casino floor with $100.

steps.title: The jackpot that's waiting to happen
steps.body:
$100 in chips. The roulette bet on a single number pays [[35-to-1|Win and you collect $35 for every $1 you bet. It feels enormous — but the number only lands about 1 spin in 38, far less often than that payout makes it feel.]] — it *feels* like a jackpot waiting to happen.

In a moment you'll sit down at the wheel, take your $100 for a spin, and watch where the chips really go.

steps.prompt: After 100 spins of a 35-to-1 bet, are you up or down?

options.label: Up — a big 35-to-1 payout will hit
Down — the bet bleeds me over time

steps.revealMessage: Lock it in, then play it out on the floor.

revealByOption.up: Tempting — and short-term it can happen, because a 35-to-1 hit is a real jackpot when it lands. But the long-run pull is downward. Let's watch variance and the trend fight it out.
revealByOption.down: That's the right instinct. A jackpot might land, but the average per spin is negative, so the longer you play the more certainly you slide. Let's see it.
revealByOption.even: It almost never lands exactly even — and the drift is downward, not flat. Play it out and watch.

steps.prompt: Hit **Spin** a few times to ride the variance — a 35-to-1 win flashes green and grows your stack. Then **Fast-forward 1,000 spins** and watch the slide. Finally tap **Be the house** and run the exact same wheel from the other side: the same math that drained you becomes steady profit.
You were *up* after 30 spins but *down* after 1,000. What changed?

options.label: Nothing changed — variance let you win early, but negative EV makes the long-run trend inevitable
The wheel went cold — your luck ran out partway through
You should have stopped sooner; playing longer is what broke even games

feedback.correct: Exactly. A negative-EV game can feel winnable for a while — that's the trap. Being up early is [[variance|How far individual results swing around the average. High variance is exactly why you can be ahead early in a losing game — but it washes out as the plays pile up.]], not skill. Flip to the house's side and the same math that drained you becomes their steady income. Same long-run engine as the insurer in Lesson 1.
feedback.incorrect: Nothing about the wheel changed — every spin had the same negative [[expected value|The average result per play if you repeated the bet forever, each outcome weighted by its chance. Here it's about −$0.26 per $5 spin: a slow, guaranteed drain.]]. Early on, variance happened to favor you; over 1,000 spins the average won out.

byOption.cold: There's no such thing as the wheel running cold — every spin is independent with the same odds. You weren't *hot* early either; that was variance. The negative EV was there the whole time.
byOption.longer: Playing longer makes a negative-EV game *worse*, not better — more spins pull your result toward the negative average. That's precisely the house's edge.

steps.hint: Did the odds of any single spin ever change? Think about variance up close versus the EV trend over the long run.

steps.title: Same engine, two sides
steps.body:
You felt both sides of one number. For you, EV per spin was negative — a slow, certain leak. For the house, the identical math runs with the sign flipped: your loss is its [[expected value|The long-run average per play. The house's is positive by exactly as much as yours is negative — that small edge, repeated millions of times, is guaranteed profit.]], collected with near-certainty.

The house doesn't win every round. It wins the *average*. That's the same long-run engine as Lesson 1's insurer — one sells hope, the other sells safety, and both let the math do the rest.
